text: A US submarine crew snuck ashore in Japan and blew up a train. Here's how they did it. In July 1945. The USS Barb was prowling off the Japanese coast when her crew spotted a railroad line running right along the shore. The trains were moving troops and supplies. Captain Eugene Fluckey approved a wild plan: Eight sailors went ashore at night, planted timed explosives on the tracks, and slipped back to the sub. Minutes later, a Japanese train hit the charge. The explosion derailed the train, killing troops and destroying cargo. This was the only confirmed ground combat action on Japan's home islands before the official invasion. The USS Barb didn't just sink ships—she proved submarines could strike on land too.

How was the food? Surprisingly good. Submarines had the best food in the Navy. That goes back to President Teddy Roosevelt. In 1901, he realized incentives were needed. So the Navy increased sub pay and gave a higher food allowance per sailor. That meant supply officers could order steaks more often. Captains fought over good cooks. Sometimes even physically. And if you were a bad cook? You didn't last long.

The typical world war 2 submarine like Pampanito carried 80 men. This was standard for all fleet boats built from 1940 forward. During the last year of the war, that number gradually increased to 85 as more equipment and specialists were added.

How did submariners sleep? Not comfortably. On World War 2 submarines, bunks were stacked three high in tiny spaces—sometimes right next to torpedoes or engines. There weren’t enough beds, so men “hot-bunked,” sharing bunks in shifts. You slept when you could, day or night, with noise, heat, and diesel fumes all around. But, sleeping was usually not an issue after a 16 hour day.

Some did experience claustrophobia. But most weren’t screened with psychology tests. Submarine duty was voluntary, so men who hated tight spaces usually didn’t sign up. The real test came on the boat. If someone froze, panicked, or couldn’t function in cramped spaces, they were quietly reassigned. In the end, the submarine itself was the screening tool.

Fear was constant on World War 2 submarines—depth charges, flooding, mechanical failures. But submarine sailors managed it through rigorous training, daily drills, and trust in their shipmates. When USS Puffer was held down for 38 hours, the men who stayed busy survived. Training replaced panic with action. Fear was real, but professionalism was stronger.

The USS Tang was America's deadliest submarine—until it killed itself. Here's what happened. October 1944. USS Tang—America's deadliest sub—fired its last torpedo at a Japanese transport. O'Kane fired at a Japanese transport. The torpedo malfunctioned—its gyroscope failed. It made a circular run and came straight back at Tang. The crew had 20 seconds. They saw their own torpedo's wake heading right for them. O'Kane ordered emergency dive, but it was too late. The torpedo hit Tang's stern. The submarine sank in 180 feet of water with 87 men aboard. Only 9 men survived, escaping using emergency breathing devices. One of them was Captain O'Kane himself. He was captured by the same Japanese ship he'd been trying to sink.

A future President of the United States was shot down in the Pacific. September 2, 1944. Twenty-year-old Navy pilot George H.W. Bush's plane exploded over Chichi Jima. He bailed out. Four hours in a life raft. Japanese boats closing in. American fighters strafing to keep them back. Then a submarine on lifeguard duty surfaced. The USS Finback. Bush spent the next month aboard. Standing watch. Dodging depth charges. Rescuing other downed pilots while destroyers hunted them. Forty-five years later, President Bush invited Finback's crew to his inauguration. The submarine's mission was sinking Japanese ships. On September 2nd, she surfaced for one American pilot instead. Want to know more about lifeguard duty? Go to http://dieselsubs.com/858

The captain was mortally wounded on the deck of his own submarine. February 7, 1943. USS Growler, caught on the surface near the Solomon Islands. Japanese patrol boat opened fire. Commander Howard Gilmore was on the bridge and wounded. Enemy ships closing in fast. The submarine had to dive. But Gilmore knew he couldn't reach the hatch in time and every second he delayed meant his crew would be in danger. He gave his final order: "Take her down." The submarine dove. Gilmore stayed on the surface. His crew survived. He didn't. He was the first submariner in World War II awarded the Medal of Honor. Would you like to know what other submariners received the Medal of Honor? Goto http://dieselsubs.com/

Being submerged felt the same as being on the surface. You were inside the pressure hull. The hull did compress slightly, but not enough to notice. Unless you were the new guy. Veterans tied a string across the hull before the dive and pulled tight. As the boat dove, the string sagged dramatically. Some new guys panicked and the old salts just laughed. The actual compression? Barely measurable. http://dieselsubs.com/

Submariners actually preferred being underwater. Here's why. On the surface? You're getting thrown around by every wave. Seasickness, rolling, crashing through swells. Miserable. But dive below 60 feet? Suddenly it's like riding in a luxury car. The ocean's chaos is all above you. Down there it's smooth, steady, quiet. You could set a coffee cup down and it wouldn't spill. The pressure? Non-existent. The pressure hull kept it all outside - you felt nothing different than sitting at home. Most guys would take a calm dive over rough surface running any day. More submarine truths at http://dieselsubs.com/

May 23rd, 1939. USS Squalus - 243 feet underwater. 33 men trapped alive. 26 already dead. The Navy rushed in an untested rescue device called the McCann Bell. It could only hold 8 men at a time. Four trips. On the last one, the cable was fraying badly. But all 33 made it out alive. It was the only mass escape from a sunken American submarine. Nine men escaped from the sunken USS Tang in 1944. Not all survived the rise to the surface. Of 52 submarines lost, over 3,500 submariners had no escape.

Did submariners feel motion when submerged? Yes, but nothing like the surface. These boats had round hulls that rolled constantly in the swells. Submariners couldn't wait to dive. At 100 feet in normal seas, the ride was glass smooth. The ocean's chaos stayed topside. But typhoons were different. Even at 200 feet, the boat would shake and bounce. Captains had to dive to 300, sometimes 400 feet, just to find calm water where the crew could eat and rest. Then back up into hell. The ocean's fury reached deeper than you think. http://dieselsubs.com/

Think you could handle submarine service? First test: Living in a 16-foot steel pipe with 79 men at 400 feet. Can't do it? You're out. But volunteers still outnumbered positions. The Submarine Force had their pick - they wanted the best. The final hurdle? Temperament. Too angry? Rejected. Too enthusiastic? Rejected. Too quiet? Rejected. They needed men who could take an even strain. Because the wrong personality could sink morale faster than depth charges could sink the boat. Only a special breed made it through. http://dieselsubs.com/

When depth charges exploded above, submarine crews faced a choice that could kill them either way. Run at 9 knots? You've got one hour before your batteries die and you're a sitting duck. Slow to 2 knots? You can hide for 48 hours... but you're barely moving. It's cubic math - double your speed, drain your battery EIGHT times faster. So when destroyers hunted overhead, commanders had to choose: burn out fast trying to escape, or go silent, creep along, and pray you could outlast them. Most terrifying math problem ever. http://dieselsubs.com/

How did they train submariners in six weeks? They didn't. During WW2, sub school was cut to weeks - or skipped entirely. The real education started on your boat. Your goal: earn your dolphins. Six months minimum for enlisted, a year for officers. You had to prove you knew every system on the boat - walk through with the Chief, exam with the captain, then dive the boat and run an attack. Why so thorough? Because there were no damage control parties on a submarine. If something went wrong in your space, YOU fixed it. Everyone needed to know everything. Your life - and 79 others - depended on it. http://dieselsubs.com/

The worst part of submarine duty? It's not what you'd guess. In WW2, it was being depth charged. Imagine being inside a steel drum while someone hits it with a sledgehammer. You hear the enemy ship above. You hear the charges hit the water. Then... the click of the detonator. And you can't move. Can't make a sound. Just wait. After the war? The smell, no showers, zero sunshine, cramped spaces. But nothing compared to hearing that click. Want more real submarine stories? Go to http://dieselsubs.com/

On a World War 2 submarine, “battle stations” was not a drill phrase. [PAUSE 0.6 sec] It meant every critical position was manned… by the best operator on board. [PAUSE 0.4 sec] The captain took the con, the sonar team listened, and the torpedo crew stood ready. [PAUSE 0.4 sec] No one asked questions. No one hesitated. [PAUSE 0.4 sec] Everyone did their job — perfectly.

Was it ever truly quiet for sleeping on a World War 2 submarine? [PAUSE 0.5 sec] Almost never. [PAUSE 0.4 sec] Diesel engines throbbed, pumps hummed, and the hull carried every sound. [PAUSE 0.4 sec] Even at rest, the boat was alive. [PAUSE 0.4 sec] So submariners learned to sleep through constant noise… [PAUSE 0.4 sec] …because silence usually meant something was wrong.

On a World War Two submarine, trust wasn’t optional — it was survival. [PAUSE 0.6] In a steel tube hundreds of feet underwater, every man depended on everyone else. [PAUSE 0.5] One mistake with sound, depth, or air could kill the entire crew. [PAUSE 0.6] They lived inches apart, sometimes worked in total darkness, and faced danger together. [PAUSE 0.5] Over time, that shared risk forged absolute trust — because if your shipmate failed, you all failed.

A good world war 2 submarine crew wasn’t built on skill alone. [PAUSE 0.4 sec] It was built on absolute trust— every man knowing the others would do their job, even in the dark. [PAUSE 0.3 sec] They trained together, lived inches apart, and learned each other’s rhythms by sound alone. [PAUSE 0.3 sec] Under depth charge, there were no heroes— only a team acting as one. [PAUSE 0.2 sec] The best crews weren’t just capable. They were unbreakably connected.

Diesel submarines weren’t just dangerous because of the enemy. They were dangerous even when no one was shooting at them. Hundreds of feet underwater… in total darkness… sealed inside a steel tube. Oxygen ran low. Batteries overheated. One leak could flood the boat in minutes. And above them, destroyers hunted — dropping depth charges powerful enough to crush steel. On a diesel submarine, survival meant skill, silence… and absolute trust in every man beside you.

What was the most dangerous job on a World War 2 submarine? Surprisingly… it wasn’t below decks. It was the lookout. He stood on the bridge, exposed to wind, waves, and enemy aircraft. Submarines spent much of the war on the surface, especially at night. That meant the lookout was the first to spot danger — and the most exposed if it arrived suddenly. No armor. No shelter. Just the open ocean. For many crews, the most dangerous place aboard was above the hull.

What did a depth charge feel like inside a World War 2 submarine? First, you heard the destroyer above you. Propellers… circling. Then the charges hit the water. Seconds later— the ocean itself exploded. The hull shuddered. Gauges rattled. Lights flickered. Men waited in silence. Not speaking. Not moving. Because noise could mean detection. And detection could mean another pattern. Veterans later said it felt like being trapped inside a steel drum while a giant hammer struck the sea around you.

Silence on a World War 2 submarine was not comfort. It was discipline. Every sound traveled through the hull. Voices were kept low. Equipment was handled carefully. Even a dropped tool could echo through the water. Enemy destroyers listened. Hydrophones searched for the smallest noise. Inside the boat, silence meant survival. Not peace. Not calm. Just men, machinery… and the constant effort to remain unheard.

What was the scariest sound inside a World War 2 submarine? It was not the engines. Not the creaking hull. Not even the sonar pings. Veterans feared one sound above all others. The faint splash of a depth charge entering the water. Then… silence. Seconds later, the explosion would shake the entire boat. Lights flickered. Gauges jumped. Men waited without speaking. Because the next sound could mean the hull had been breached. Inside a diesel submarine, sound was not just noise. It was survival.

Did submariners actually hear enemy ships above them? Yes, and sometimes, far too clearly. Inside a World War 2 diesel submarine, sound traveled through the hull. Propellers. Machinery. The steady rhythm of screws turning overhead. When a destroyer passed above, the noise was unmistakable. A deep rumble felt through steel and bone. Men froze in place. No talking and no unnecessary movement. Sonarmen listened through headphones, tracking every sound contact. And when the propellers slowed… everyone understood the danger. Silence wasn’t peace. It was survival.

Why was there red lighting in some spaces on a World War 2 submarine? It was not for atmosphere. It protected night vision. Bright white light could leave a sailor nearly blind when stepping into darkness. Red lighting prevented that sudden blindness and allowed men to move to the bridge or periscope without losing vision. This was critical for lookouts, who searched dark horizons for the first sign of enemy ships. Red light also reduced visible light escaping the boat at night. Much of the submarine was rigged for red lighting. Not comfort. But readiness in the dark.

What did the crew do while waiting for depth charges? They waited in silence. Men stayed at their stations. Voices were kept low. Every movement was deliberate. The boat would go deep and slow. Engines were handled carefully to reduce noise. Above them, destroyers searched the water, listening for any sound from below. Inside the hull, there was no panic. Only discipline. Only focus. Some men watched gauges. Others listened through headphones. Everyone understood the same truth. When the charges fell, there was nothing to do but remain quiet, steady, and trust the boat — and each other.

Did submarines deliver spies behind enemy lines? Yes. And it was done in near total silence. At night, a World War Two diesel submarine could surface off a hostile coast and quietly launch rubber boats into the darkness. Inside were commandos, radio operators, and intelligence agents heading into occupied territory. The crew spoke softly. No lights were shown. The boat had to remain undetected. Once the agents paddled ashore, the submarine submerged and slipped away. For the crew, it was another patrol. For the men left behind, the real mission had only just begun.

Periscope depth was dangerous for a World War 2 submarine. Enemy aircraft and ships could detect the boat. In deeper water, a submarine could remain hidden. But observation required risk. To search the horizon, the captain had to raise the periscope and hold the submarine at a shallow depth. Inside the submarine, electric motors hummed softly, and unnecessary movements were kept to a minimum. The margin for error was small. Too high, and the submarine could broach the surface. Too noisy, and it could be heard. Periscope depth was an exposed position where discipline and silence meant survival.
